# Veteran families with complex needs: a qualitative study of the veterans’ support system

[*BMC Health Services Research*](/)
**volume 22**, Article number: 74 (2022)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

8101 Accesses

10 Citations

1 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2/metrics)

## Abstract

### Background

Families with complex needs face significant challenges accessing and navigating health and social services. For veteran families, these challenges are exacerbated by interactions between military and civilian systems of care, and the density of the veterans’ non-profit sector. This qualitative study was designed to gather rich, detailed information on complex needs in veteran families; and explore service providers’ and families’ experiences of accessing and navigating the veterans’ support system.

### Methods

The study comprised participant background questionnaires (*n* = 34), focus groups with frontline service providers (*n* = 18), and one-on-one interviews with veteran families (*n* = 16) in Australia. The semi-structured focus groups and interviews were designed to gather rich, detailed information on four study topics: (i) health and wellbeing needs in veteran families; (ii) service-access barriers and facilitators; (iii) unmet needs and gaps in service provision; and (iv) practical solutions for improving service delivery. The study recruited participants who could best address the focus on veteran families with complex needs. The questionnaire data was used to describe relevant characteristics of the participant sample. The focus groups and interviews were audio-recorded, transcribed, and a reflexive thematic analysis was conducted to identify patterns of shared meaning in the qualitative data.

### Results

Both service providers and families found the veterans’ support system difficult to access and navigate. System fragmentation was perceived to impede care coordination, and delay access to holistic care for veteran families with complex needs. The medico-legal aspects of compensation and rehabilitation processes were perceived to harm veteran identity, and undermine health and wellbeing outcomes. Recovery-oriented practice was viewed as a way to promote veteran independence and self-management. Participants expressed a strong preference for family-centred care that was informed by an understanding of military lifestyle and culture.

### Conclusion

The health and wellbeing needs of veteran families intensify during the transition from full-time military service to civilian environments, and service- or reintegration-related difficulties may emerge (or persist) for a significant period of time thereafter. Veteran families with complex needs are unduly burdened by care coordination demands. There is a pressing need for high-quality implementation studies that evaluate initiatives for integrating fragmented systems of care.

[Peer Review reports](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2/peer-review)

Military to civilian questionnaire: a measure of postdeployment community reintegration difficulty among veterans using Department of Veterans’ affairs medical care. J Trauma Stress. 2011;24(6):660–70.

BMC Psychiatry. 2020;20:531

The losses that characterise the reintegration experience have direct and indirect effects for veterans’ family members, particularly their partners and children [[11](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR11 "Elnitsky CA, Fisher MP, Blevins CL. Military service member and veteran reintegration: a conceptual analysis, unified definition, and key domains. Front Psychol. 2017;8:369 
                  https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2017.00369
                  
                ."), [16](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR16 "Daraganova G, Smart D, Romaniuk H. Family wellbeing study (Part 1). Families of current and ex-serving ADF members: health and wellbeing. [Internet] Canberra, ACT: The Department of Defence and the Department of Veterans’ Affairs; 2018 [updated unknown; cited 2021 Jun 3]. Available from: 
                  https://www.dva.gov.au/documents-and-publications/family-wellbeing-study
                  
                ")]. While many contemporary veterans successfully navigate this transition; approximately 50% experience service- or reintegration-related difficulties that require low- to high-intensity intervention (i.e., self-management through to complex care coordination) [[9](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR9 "Sayer NA, Noorbaloochi S, Frazier P, Carlson K, Gravely A, Murdoch M. Reintegration problems and treatment interests among Iraq and Afghanistan combat veterans receiving VA medical care. Psychiatr Serv. 2010;61(6):589–97 
                  https://doi.org/10.1176/appi.ps.61.6.589
                  
                ."), [17](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR17 "Van Hooff M, Lawrence-Wood E, Hodson S, Sadler N, Benassi H, Hansen C, et al. Mental health and wellbeing transition study: mental health prevalence. [Internet] Canberra, ACT: The Department of Defence and the Department of Veterans’ Affairs; 2018 [updated unknown; cited 2021 Jun 3]. Available from: 
                  https://www.dva.gov.au/documents-and-publications/mental-health-prevalence-report
                  
                ")]. This paper focuses on former-serving veteran families with complex needs [[18](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR18 "Rankin J, Reagan S. Meeting complex needs: the future of social care. London: Turning Point; 2004. 
                  https://www.ippr.org/publications/meeting-complex-needsthe-future-of-social-care
                  
                ")].

### Complex needs

Families with complex needs have severe, persistent, interconnected difficulties that span multiple domains of functioning [[18](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR18 "Rankin J, Reagan S. Meeting complex needs: the future of social care. London: Turning Point; 2004. 
                  https://www.ippr.org/publications/meeting-complex-needsthe-future-of-social-care
                  
                ")]. Such families face significant challenges accessing and navigating the services required to address the breadth and depth of their needs [[18](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR18 "Rankin J, Reagan S. Meeting complex needs: the future of social care. London: Turning Point; 2004. 
                  https://www.ippr.org/publications/meeting-complex-needsthe-future-of-social-care
                  
                ")]. In 2015, estimates of focal health and wellbeing needs in a national, stratified sample[Footnote 1](#Fn1) of a recently discharged cohort (2010–2015) of Australian veterans indicated: 58.7% had one or more doctor-diagnosed medical conditions; 46.4% had a formally-diagnosed mental health condition (55.5% of whom had at least one co-morbid mental health condition); 43.7% reported a period of unemployment lasting at least 3 months since discharge; 43.6% had a service-related compensation claim accepted; 31.7% were not in paid employment; 28.3% had no post-secondary education; 23.0% were not in a relationship; 20.4% had been medically discharged; 7.2% were not receiving income; 6.2% were not in stable housing (last 2 months); and 2.9% reported having been arrested, convicted (2.1%), or imprisoned (0.07%) since transition [[17](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR17 "Van Hooff M, Lawrence-Wood E, Hodson S, Sadler N, Benassi H, Hansen C, et al. Mental health and wellbeing transition study: mental health prevalence. [Internet] Canberra, ACT: The Department of Defence and the Department of Veterans’ Affairs; 2018 [updated unknown; cited 2021 Jun 3]. Available from: 
                  https://www.dva.gov.au/documents-and-publications/mental-health-prevalence-report
                  
                ")]. The report did not provide estimates of the percentage of the cohort with complex needs spanning the aforementioned health and socioeconomic domains of functioning.

### Systems of care

Similar to the United States, Australia’s health and social care system is relatively complex; characterised by multiple payer-provider schemes that exist across multiple levels of government and multiple sectors (e.g., the Medicare Benefits Scheme [MBS]; the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme [PBS]; the National Disability Insurance Scheme [NDIS]; private insurers; public compensation and rehabilitation schemes; state-funded Hospital and Health Services [HHSs]; federally-funded Primary Health Networks [PHNs]; and other public, private and non-profit agencies). For veteran families, the challenges of service navigation are compounded by the military system of care (e.g., the Department of Defence [Defence]; the Australian Defence Force [ADF]; Defence Member and Family Support [DMFS; formerly the Defence Community Organisation]; the Commonwealth Superannuation Scheme [CSC]; the Department of Veterans’ Affairs [DVA]; the Repatriation Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme [RPBS]); and the density of the Ex-Service Organisation (ESO) non-profit sector. In 2015, more than 3000 charities and trusts were providing support to Australian veteran families; of these, over 500 entities identified veteran families as the sole beneficiaries of their services [[19](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR19 "Aspen Foundation. Ex-Service Organisation (ESO) mapping project: final report. [Internet] Canberra: Aspen Foundation; 2016 [updated unknown; cited 2021 Jun 3]. Available from: 
                  https://www.aspenfoundation.org.au/esomp
                  
                ")]. Gill et al. [[20](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR20 "Gill GF, Bain R, Seidl I. Supporting Australia’s new veterans. Aust Fam Physician. 2016;45(3):102–6 
                  https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2016/march/supporting-australia%E2%80%99s-new-veterans/
                  
                .")] provide a detailed summary of the interface between Australian military and civilian systems of care, and the services and supports available to veteran families across the transition pathways from military to civilian environments.

A series of recent government inquiries have identified a need to better integrate the many services and programs offered to Australian veteran families [[15](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR15 "Australian Institute of Health and Welfare. Serving and ex-serving Australian Defence Force members who have served since 1985: suicide monitoring 2001 to 2019 [Internet] Canberra, ACT: Australian Government; 2021 [updated 2021 Sep 29; cited 2021 Oct 14]. Available from: 
                  https://www.aihw.gov.au/reports/veterans/serving-and-ex-serving-adf-suicide-monitoring-2001/contents/about
                  
                "), [21](#ref-CR21 "The Senate Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade References Committee. The constant battle: suicide by veterans. [Internet] Canberra, ACT: Australian Government; 2017 [updated 2017 Aug; cited 2021 Jun 3]. Available from: 
                  https://www.dva.gov.au/about-us/overview/reporting/reviews-and-reports/constant-battle
                  
                "),[22](#ref-CR22 "Cornall R. Veterans’ advocacy and support services scoping study report: a modern professional sustainable service for Australian veterans and their families. [Internet] Canberra, ACT: Australian Government; 2018 [updated 2018 Dec; cited 2021 Jun 3]. Available from: 
                  https://www.dva.gov.au/documents-and-publications/veterans-advocacy-and-support-services-scoping-study
                  
                "),[23](#ref-CR23 "Collie A. The mental health impacts of compensation claim assessment processes. [Internet] Melbourne, VIC: Insurance Work and Health Group, School of Public Health and Preventive Medicine, Faculty of Medicine Nursing and Health Sciences, Monash University; 2019 [updated 2019 Mar 5; cited 2021 Jun 3]. Available from: 
                  https://www.dva.gov.au/mental-health-impacts-compensation-claim-assessment-processes
                  
                "),[24](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR24 "Productivity Commission. A better way to support veterans. Report no. 93. [Internet] Canberra, ACT: Australian Government; 2019 [updated 2019 Jun 27; cited 2021 Jun 3]. Available from: 
                  https://www.pc.gov.au/inquiries/completed/veterans/report
                  
                ")]. However, very little research has examined their experiences of navigating military and civilian systems of care. One qualitative study by Muir [[25](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR25 "Muir S. Family wellbeing study (Part 2). Military family approaches to managing transition to civilian life. [Internet] Canberra, ACT: The Department of Defence and the Department of Veterans’ Affairs; 2018 [updated unknown; cited 2021 Jun 3]. Available from: 
                  https://www.dva.gov.au/documents-and-publications/family-wellbeing-study
                  
                ")], commissioned by DVA as part of a joint initiative with Defence, included a component that asked family members (i.e., partners, parents, and adult children; *n* = 25) about strategies for improving the civilian reintegration process. Family members reported a need for: (i) better discharge planning for veterans and families (predominantly more individually-tailored assistance for veterans who were medically discharging or suffering from severe/ongoing health conditions); (ii) clearer communication with families that was more targeted to their specific circumstances; and (iii) service provision that was more proactive [[25](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR25 "Muir S. Family wellbeing study (Part 2). Military family approaches to managing transition to civilian life. [Internet] Canberra, ACT: The Department of Defence and the Department of Veterans’ Affairs; 2018 [updated unknown; cited 2021 Jun 3]. Available from: 
                  https://www.dva.gov.au/documents-and-publications/family-wellbeing-study
                  
                ")].

A comprehensive understanding of the needs, values, and preferences of veteran families is required to tailor a system integration solution that can be implemented and sustained in the real-world setting [[26](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR26 "Janamian T, Crossland L, Jackson CL. Embracing value co-creation in primary care services research: a framework for success. Med J Aust. 2016;204(7):S5–11 
                  https://doi.org/10.5694/mja16.00112
                  
                .")]. This qualitative study was designed to gather rich, detailed information on complex needs in veteran families; and explore service providers’ and families’ experiences of accessing and navigating the veterans’ support system. The study is described according to the COnsolidated criteria for REporting Qualitative research (COREQ) [[27](/articles/10.1186/s12913-021-07368-2#ref-CR27 "Tong A, Sainsbury P, Craig J. Consolidated criteria for reporting qualitative research (COREQ): a 32-item checklist for interviews and focus groups. Int J Qual Health Care. 2007;19(6):349–57 
                  https://doi.org/10.1093/intqhc/mzm042
                  
                .")].

## Method

### Design

Phenomenology was selected as the research framework for the study due to its focus on the human subjective experience: the philosophical basis of the approach emphasises participants’ lived experience of the study topic(s), views their experiences as conscious and subjective, and focuses on describing (rather than explaining) the e

URL: https://www.veterans.senate.gov/services/files/1DB11AF2-B4C8-4029-BE5A-3DABD5D1D07B

SUMMARY:
Some veterans are denied access, and there are backlogs in scanning community care documents, causing delays and affecting patient outcomes.

#### Veterans Caregiving Resources

# Veteran Health Care Challenges: Navigating the Complexities of Treatment and Support

###### By

###### TCARE

Veterans face unique health challenges due to their military service, including physical injuries, mental health conditions, and long-term chronic illnesses. Despite various programs designed to support veterans, navigating the complexities of treatment and accessing the right care can be daunting. From understanding eligibility for Veterans Affairs (VA) health services to finding the right treatment plans for conditions like PTSD or traumatic brain injuries (TBIs), many veterans and their caregivers struggle to get the care they need.

In this article, weâll explore the common healthcare challenges that veterans face, the complexities of the VA healthcare system, and practical strategies for navigating treatment and support services. Whether you are a veteran, a family member, or a caregiver, this guide aims to help you better understand how to access and maximize available resources.

### **Unique Health Care Challenges Faced by Veterans**

Veterans often face a range of physical, mental, and emotional health challenges that stem from their service. These issues can be long-lasting and require specialized care. Below are some of the most common health challenges that veterans experience:

#### **Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)**

PTSD is one of the most well-known mental health conditions affecting veterans. It occurs after exposure to traumatic events during military service, such as combat, natural disasters, or life-threatening situations. Symptoms of PTSD can include flashbacks, nightmares, severe anxiety, and hypervigilance, making daily life difficult for veterans and their families.

#### **Traumatic Brain Injuries (TBIs)**

TBIs are common among veterans, particularly those who have served in combat zones. These injuries are caused by blasts, falls, or blunt force trauma and can lead to cognitive impairments, memory issues, and changes in behavior. Depending on the severity of the injury, veterans may require long-term rehabilitation or cognitive therapy.

#### **Chronic Pain and Physical Injuries**

Physical injuries, including musculoskeletal problems, chronic pain, and loss of limb function, are prevalent among veterans due to the physical demands of military service. These injuries often lead to long-term pain management needs and reduced mobility, requiring specialized care and rehabilitation.

#### **Mental Health Issues Beyond PTSD**

In addition to PTSD, many veterans experience other mental health conditions such as depression, anxiety, and substance use disorders. These conditions may arise from the stresses of military life, reintegration into civilian life, or a combination of factors. Veterans often need both psychiatric care and social support to manage these conditions.

#### **Exposure to Hazardous Materials**

Many veterans, especially those who served in Iraq and Afghanistan, were exposed to hazardous materials, such as burn pits, asbestos, or chemical agents, which can lead to long-term health complications like respiratory diseases and cancer. Veterans exposed to Agent Orange during the Vietnam War are also at risk for various cancers and other diseases.

#### **Homelessness and Economic Hardships**

Veterans are disproportionately affected by homelessness and economic instability, often due to the challenges of transitioning to civilian life and dealing with the aftermath of service-related injuries or mental health issues. Homeless veterans often have difficulty accessing consistent health care, further complicating their treatment.

### **The Complexities of Navigating the VA Health Care System**

The [U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs](https://www.va.gov//) (VA) is the primary provider of health care for veterans, offering a wide range of services, including hospital care, mental health services, rehabilitation, and long-term care. While the VA provides valuable support, many veterans find the system challenging to navigate due to its complex eligibility requirements, long wait times, and geographic limitations.

#### **Eligibility for VA Health Care**

Eligibility for VA health care is based on several factors, including military service history, discharge status, income, and the presence of a service-connected disability. Not all veterans qualify for VA health care, and the level of benefits available varies depending on the veteran's priority group, which is assigned based on these factors.

#### **Long Wait Times and Access Issues**

One of the most significant challenges veterans face is long wait times to receive care, especially for specialty services like mental health care, orthopedic care, or cancer treatment. In some cases, veterans may wait months for appointments, exacerbating their health problems.

#### **Navigating the VAâs Referral and Authorization Process**

Veterans requiring specialized care or services not available at their local VA facility may be referred to non-VA providers. The [**Veteranâs Choice Program**](https://www.publichealth.va.gov/exposures/publications/oef-oif-ond/post-9-11-vet-fall-2015/veterans-choice-program.asp) and the newer **Community Care Program** were established to address the gap in care availability. However, the process of getting a referral and ensuring authorization for treatment can be confusing and time-consuming.

#### **Mental Health Care Stigma**

Despite the availability of mental health services through the VA, many veterans are reluctant to seek help due to the stigma associated with mental illness. This can lead to untreated conditions such as PTSD, depression, and substance use disorders, which often worsen over time.

### **Strategies for Navigating Veteran Health Care Challenges**

While the challenges of accessing health care can be overwhelming, there are strategies and resources that can help veterans and their families navigate the system more effectively.

#### **Understand Your Eligibility and Benefits**

The first step in maximizing VA health care is understanding your eligibility and the benefits available to you. Take time to review the VAâs priority groups, eligibility requirements, and benefit tiers.

#### **Utilize Veterans Service Officers (VSOs)**

Veterans Service Officers are a valuable resource for veterans and their families. They offer free assistance in filing claims, appealing decisions, and accessing benefits. VSOs work for organizations such as the **Disabled American Veterans (DAV)**, the **American Legion**, or state veterans departments, and they can help veterans navigate the often-confusing VA system.

#### **Take Advantage of Community Care Programs**

If the VA cannot provide timely care, veterans can utilize the **Veteranâs Choice Program** or the **VA Community Care Program**, which allows veterans to seek care outside of the VA network under certain circumstances.

#### **Advocate for Mental Health Support**

Mental health conditions such as PTSD, depression, and anxiety are common among veterans. It's crucial for veterans to seek help and address these issues early on. The VA offers numerous mental health services, including therapy, psychiatric care, support groups, and counseling.

#### **Plan Ahead for Long-Term and Specialized Care**

Veterans with chronic conditions or serious injuries should plan ahead for long-term care needs. The VA offers **Home-Based Primary Care (HBPC)**, which provides in-home care for veterans who have difficulty traveling to VA facilities. Veterans should also explore **respite care**, **hospice**, and **nursing home services** offered through the VA for those who need ongoing assistance.

#### **Use Technology for Easier Access**

The VA has expanded its use of technology to help veterans access care more easily. **My HealtheVet** is an online portal that allows veterans to manage appointments, refill prescriptions, and communicate with healthcare providers. Additionally, **Telehealth** services offer virtual appointments for mental health care, primary care, and certain specialty services, reducing the need for travel.

### **Conclusion: Navigating Veteran Health Care Successfully**

While the VA health care system can be complex, understanding how to navigate its services is crucial for ensuring veterans receive the care they need. By becoming familiar with the resources available, advocating for timely care, and working with professionals like Veterans Service Officers, veterans and their families can overcome the challenges of accessing treatment and improve their overall health and well-being.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Veterans commonly face multifaceted health issues, including PTSD, chronic pain, and long-term injuries, requiring specialized care and integrated treatment plans to address their unique challenges.

Many veterans encounter significant barriers when navigating the VA healthcare system, including long wait times, complex eligibility requirements, and fragmentation between military and civilian care systems.

Personalized treatment approaches are vital for veterans, as they address individual health histories and preferences, ultimately enhancing recovery outcomes and ensuring a holistic approach to their care.
